column72: If an AI system were used to screen my job application, I would probably feel a mix of cautious optimism and concern.  On the positive side, AI screening could make the process more efficient and potentially more consistent. A well-designed system might reduce some forms of human bias, evaluate applications based on clear criteria, and process large numbers of candidates fairly.  However, I would also feel concerned about transparency and bias. I might worry about whether the system could misinterpret parts of my experience, overlook nontraditional career paths, or rely on hidden correlations (like name, background, or writing style) that could disadvantage certain applicants. It would also be frustrating if I were rejected without understanding why, especially if the reasoning isn’t explainable.  Overall, I’d feel more comfortable if:  The AI were used as a support tool rather than the sole decision-maker.  There were human oversight and review.  The process were transparent, with clear evaluation criteria.  There were safeguards against bias and discrimination.   Knowing those protections are in place would make the experience feel more fair and trustworthy.
column77: When reading the definition of AI, I felt a mix of curiosity and reflection.  On one hand, it’s impressive to see how AI is described as systems that can perform tasks like understanding language, recognizing patterns, and generating content — things that once seemed uniquely human. That highlights how far the technology has progressed.  On the other hand, the definition also feels grounding. It reminds me that AI systems don’t truly “understand” the world the way humans do; they learn patterns from data and make predictions based on probabilities. That distinction is important, especially when thinking about bias, responsibility, and how these systems should be used.  Overall, the definition made me both excited about AI’s capabilities and thoughtful about its limitations and impact.
column79: During the interaction, I read through each section about language models and reflected on what I learned.  I summarized how models like BERT are trained by predicting missing words and how they learn statistical patterns from large amounts of text. I also explained how these models can pick up on regional patterns (like differences between Texas and New York) and social associations (such as connections between names, gender, and occupations).  In the later sections, I reflected on the risks of bias in language models, including how they can reinforce stereotypes, and discussed approaches to reducing bias, such as training with gender-swapped sentences.  Overall, I engaged with each section by summarizing key ideas, considering their implications, and sharing thoughtful reflections on how language models learn patterns and how bias can be addressed.

column72: If an AI system were used to screen my job application, I would probably feel a mix of cautious optimism and concern.  On the positive side, AI screening could make the process more efficient and potentially more consistent. A well-designed system might reduce some forms of human bias, evaluate applications based on clear criteria, and process large numbers of candidates fairly.  However, I would also feel concerned about transparency and bias. I might worry about whether the system could misinterpret parts of my experience, overlook nontraditional career paths, or rely on hidden correlations (like name, background, or writing style) that could disadvantage certain applicants. It would also be frustrating if I were rejected without understanding why, especially if the reasoning isn’t explainable.  Overall, I’d feel more comfortable if:  The AI were used as a support tool rather than the sole decision-maker.  There were human oversight and review.  The process were transparent, with clear evaluation criteria.  There were safeguards against bias and discrimination.   Knowing those protections are in place would make the experience feel more fair and trustworthy.
column77: When reading the definition of AI, I felt a mix of curiosity and reflection.  On one hand, it’s impressive to see how AI is described as systems that can perform tasks like understanding language, recognizing patterns, and generating content — things that once seemed uniquely human. That highlights how far the technology has progressed.  On the other hand, the definition also feels grounding. It reminds me that AI systems don’t truly “understand” the world the way humans do; they learn patterns from data and make predictions based on probabilities. That distinction is important, especially when thinking about bias, responsibility, and how these systems should be used.  Overall, the definition made me both excited about AI’s capabilities and thoughtful about its limitations and impact.
column79: During the interaction, I read through each section about language models and reflected on what I learned.  I summarized how models like BERT are trained by predicting missing words and how they learn statistical patterns from large amounts of text. I also explained how these models can pick up on regional patterns (like differences between Texas and New York) and social associations (such as connections between names, gender, and occupations).  In the later sections, I reflected on the risks of bias in language models, including how they can reinforce stereotypes, and discussed approaches to reducing bias, such as training with gender-swapped sentences.  Overall, I engaged with each section by summarizing key ideas, considering their implications, and sharing thoughtful reflections on how language models learn patterns and how bias can be addressed.

column79: I learned how do the Large Language Models learn how to fill in the blanks in words. They assign some amount of probability to every word they consider filling in the blank space. During the learning process BERT (the first language model) uncovered some hidden corelations behind people average lifespan length based on their name. It was also biased towards certain words, assigning them more to a certain biological sex. People tried debiasing the model by balancing the data sets with equal amount of gendered nouns, however it didnt work perfectly and minimally decreased the bias. It is speculated that in the future, as language models get more developed, they may be able to debias themselfes without human interaction with them. Overall it was an interesting and valuable experience.

column71: To some extent, yes. Understanding how a model works does help build trust, because it allows you to evaluate whether its reasoning makes sense.
